articles.title: Descend Into a Volcano's Magma Chamber—No Lava Required
articles.summary: Imagine stepping into a volcano's heart—not with lava, but with walls painted in bronze and indigo. In Iceland, Thríhnúkagígur is the only place on Earth where you can descend into an empty magma chamber. The volcano hasn't erupted for 4,500 years, and its magma mysteriously vanished, possibly sucked back into the crust. Visitors take an open elevator 700 feet down to explore a cavern bigger than a football field. The colors come from microbes and sulfur gases. Scientists aren't sure why the chamber is empty, but it's safe—no eruption expected. Would you dare to go?

articles.title: Could a Dam Between Alaska and Russia Save a Critical Ocean Current?
articles.summary: Imagine a dam stretching 24 miles across the Bering Strait between Alaska and Russia. Scientists say it might help preserve the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), a crucial ocean current that keeps Europe warm. But the idea is risky: the dam could harm wildlife, Indigenous communities, and shipping, and might even backfire. A new study in Science Advances models the effects, finding that the dam could strengthen AMOC in some scenarios but accelerate its collapse in others. It's not a straightforward solution, but it highlights how desperate we are to combat climate change.

articles.title: The Great Stork Derby: When a Rich Man's Joke Turned Into a Baby-Making Contest
articles.summary: In 1926, eccentric Toronto lawyer Charles Vance Millar died and left a bizarre will: the mother who gave birth to the most children in ten years would inherit a fortune worth nearly 9 million Canadian dollars today. This sparked the "Great Stork Derby," a media frenzy that divided Canada during the Great Depression. The contest pitted families against each other, often along ethnic lines, as newspapers highlighted rivals like Italian immigrant Grace Bagnato and "native-born" Florence Brown. The derby ignited debates about morality, class, and immigration. It ended in 1936 with four mothers splitting the prize.
